topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box was heavy, smelling faintly of old paper and lavender. Leo dragged it into the attic, the floorboards groaning in protest beneath his sneakers. He hadn't expected this. His mother, usually so neat, had apparently kept every single worksheet, every essay, from elementary school. He wrestled the lid open, and the sight inside was overwhelming: stacks upon stacks of colorful paper, neatly tied with faded ribbons. A tight knot formed in Leo’s chest. *Why?* he thought, his jaw clenching so hard his teeth ached. He picked up a crumpled drawing from third grade, the ink smudged, and felt a sudden, intense urge to just throw the whole collection back into the box and forget it existed. He slammed the lid shut, the sound echoing in the dusty silence.

[story 2]

"Honestly, Mom, why is everything organized like this?" Mark sighed, running a hand through his already messy hair. He was supposed to be helping clear out the garage, but instead, he found the forgotten storage bin. Inside, nestled between old tools, was a veritable archive of his academic life. Hundreds of assignments. Mark stared at the sheer volume, a slow, simmering frustration building behind his eyes. He picked up a particularly dense history report, the weight of the paper feeling like an accusation. He wanted to just leave it there, let the clutter settle, but he couldn't bring himself to discard it. He carefully placed the report back down, his movements stiff, a clear refusal to engage with the task at hand.

[story 3]

I found the trunk tucked behind the linen closet, and the sheer volume of schoolwork hit me like a physical wall. It was an unbelievable hoard of papers, every doodle, every graded test, every homework sheet from my childhood. I leaned against the doorframe, my arms crossed tightly over my chest. A deep sense of resistance washed over me. *This is unnecessary,* I thought, the words a sharp, internal critique. I looked at a brightly colored worksheet from kindergarten, and a wave of sheer refusal washed over me. I didn't want to sort it, didn't want to understand it, didn't want to acknowledge it. I just wanted the box gone, to disappear.
